next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Lantern Quay announced itself by smell before the lamps showed: fish skin blistering over charcoal, rice going sweet under lid, lamp oil cut with orange peel so the whole wet village smelled like somebody stubbornly insisting on comfort in weather that did not deserve it. Iven had been wet so long his shirt felt grown into him. The first breath of cooking hit his empty stomach hard enough that he had to sit down on the gunwale and pretend he only meant to retie the mooring rope.

The quay came out of the rain a little at a time. Stilt houses. Smoke slipping under the eaves instead of up. Prayer bowls hung on nails to dry, copper and clay and lacquered gourd, their rims clicking together in the wind like teeth. Children had already stolen half a dozen and were racing them through a runnel beside the steps, crouched in the mud and shrieking every time one tipped and filled.

Riva brought the ferry in with the stiff mean care she used when exhaustion was one bad decision away from turning into temper. Daro was sitting near the bow with his shoulders fallen in, beautiful and used up. Lale had Nami's bad hand in her lap, changing the cloth around the cut with those indigo-stained nails that always made her look cleaner than anybody else even when she had gone two days without proper sleep. Nami kept trying to look over her own bandage as if curiosity might lower the fever.

People were already coming down the steps before the hull thudded the posts. Not pilgrims. Not gawkers. Just Lantern Quay folk in rolled trousers and aprons and rain capes, moving with the brisk...
open_hooks_to_carry: Living townspeople begin walking toward water under the seeds' influence.
Riva must split attention between the route and rescue.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...lf it was there. "Will the marks really tell her when to turn?"

"If she pays attention." Paska bent to pick the ledger up from beside the cat and held it out to Lale. "Skin's honest longer than paper. That's the lesson. Try not to waste it."

Lale took the book with her unmarked hand. The gap in the binding felt uglier now that it had a cause she could not blame on anybody else. She tucked the ledger under her arm, careful of the fresh bandage, and followed the others out onto the walkway.

The market had resumed itself below them because markets always did. A man was gutting silver fish under a patched awning. A girl with scarlet glass bangles was haggling over lamp oil as if flood and corpse and saint family had nothing to do with the price of ordinary life. Rain began again in a fine slant, not enough to drive anyone indoors. The water under the pilings looked blacker than ink.

Riva went ahead toward the mooring lane. Daro lingered half a step behind Lale, thinking. Iven dropped to the rear. Nami moved wherever there was room, all alertness and wet hair and dangerous openness. The family kept its old shape even now, each person falling where habit and injury placed them.

Lale felt the wrapped marks pull when she closed her hand around the rail. The sting was immediate and exact. Good. Let the body keep the account for once. Let it refuse neatness. Let the phrase sit where she could not lock it in a drawer.

Sixth mouth.

It had been ink once. Then paper. Then absence. Now it moved warm under her own skin as she stepped toward Inkwater and the waiting turn of the tide.
state_deltas_required: Sleepwalking townspeople, infected floodwater, Nami's injury, and a half-open third channel.
The threat reaches the living, Nami is wounded and feverish, and delay becomes lethal rather than merely reputational.
